title: Lesson 340
transcript: Bye. There's one more really important piece that we have to discuss related to biometric systems, and that is templates. So let's first start with the definition, what is a template? A template is essentially hashed biometric data. So the idea here is that You never want to store, like a good biometric system should never store someone's raw biometric data. Because imagine someone broke into that system and stole all of that raw biometric data. They would have someone's fingerprints or their iris pattern or something like that. And that would be pretty catastrophic for the individual. If your password gets stolen, it's not the end of the world, right? Just go and memorize a new password. If someone steals your fingerprint data, What do you do now? Like you can't just grow new fingerprints or you can't grow a new eyeball, right? So this is a massive problem. And this is why good biometric systems will never just store the raw biometric data. Essentially what the system will do, it's not quite hashing, but it's relying on similar techniques. The biometric system will essentially hash the biometric data, generating what is known as a template. So you can kind of think of a template as like a hash value of biometric data. And the whole point of this is that if an attacker were to break into a biometric system and steal all these templates, They would not be able to use this template to figure out the original biometric data, the facial recognition data, the iris or retinal pattern, the fingerprint pattern, the vein pattern. The template is essentially hash data. It's one way. Now the next piece we have to discuss here is the two major ways that templates can be used. The first thing we're going to talk about here is what's known as a one-to-n search. So imagine this scenario. You walk up to a door and you just put your fingerprint on a fingerprint reader to unlock the door. What's happening here? Well, the fingerprint reader is capturing your biometric data, your fingerprint data, and it's generating a new template. That's the one that you see listed here. So the fingerprint reader on the door has captured your fingerprint data and generated a new template. Now the door doesn't know who you are. So what needs to happen next is that template that's just been generated from your fingerprint, the system now has to search through a database of existing templates to see if there is a reasonably close match. And if there's a template in the database that reasonably closely matches, then the system will go, aha, I know who this is. This is Rob. And so a one to end search where a template is generated and then used to search through a database for a reasonably close match This one-to-end search is used for identification, right? Because when you just walk up to your door and scan your fingerprint, the system has no idea who you are. So primarily what's happening here in a one-to-end search is identification. The other major use case for templates is in a one-to-one comparison. So here's how this might work. Imagine you walk up to your laptop, you type in your username and your password and you hit enter. The system has now identified you and actually verified your identity through one factor of authentication. But maybe the system, right, it's a fairly secure system, so maybe you want to do a second factor of authentication. Here's how this would work, right? So you type in your username, you type in your password, hit enter, and the system says, okay, before I let you get access, Rob, please put your finger on the fingerprint reader. So I put my finger on the fingerprint reader. The system captures my biometric data, generates a template. Now, because the system already knows who I am, because it's already looked, you know, I've already identified myself by typing my username. The system knows who I am. So the identification's already happened. So what the system is going to do is go and look in the database, look up the template that it already has for me, and compare the template that was on file for me to the template that was just generated from my fingerprint and compare them one to one. And if there's a close enough match, then the system will use this as a second factor of authentication. The system will say, aha, hey, this is in fact Rob because the templates reasonably match. So this is being used as a second factor of authentication. So remember those kind of major two use cases of templates. A one-to-end search is used for identification. A one-to-one comparison is used for authentication. Bye.
